items.title: Effective Code Reviews with Conventional Comments • Paul Slaughter & Adrienne Braganza
items.author: GOTO Conferences
items.content:
<article>This interview was recorded for GOTO Unscripted.
https://gotopia.tech

Read the full transcription of this interview here
 (https://gotopia.tech/articles/372) 
Paul Slaughter - Staff Fullstack Engineer at GitLab & Creator of Conventional Comments
Adrienne Braganza Tacke - Senior Developer Advocate at Viam Robotics & Author of "Looks Good To Me: Constructive Code Reviews"

RESOURCES
Paul
https://x.com/souldzin
https://github.com/souldzin
https://gitlab.com/pslaughter
https://gitlab.com/souldzin
https://souldzin.com

Adrienne
https://bsky.app/profile/abt.bsky.social
https://x.com/AdrienneTacke
https://github.com/AdrienneTacke
https://www.linkedin.com/in/adriennetacke
https://www.instagram.com/adriennetacke
https://www.adrienne.io
https://blog.adrienne.io

Links
https://conventionalcomments.org

DESCRIPTION
Can "Conventional Comments" transform code reviews from frustrating experiences into productive collaborations?

Paul Slaughter shares his experience developing and practicing "Conventional Comments", a structured approach to improving code review communications through labeled feedback. The conversation explores clear communication patterns with labels (e.g. 'suggestion:', 'issue:' or 'question:').

Paul and Adrienne discuss the importance of empathy in the review process, the balance between politeness and efficiency, and how GitLab's Code Review Weekly Workshops have helped normalize review experiences across their remote teams. The interview highlights that effective code reviews depend not just on technical evaluations but on thoughtful communication that acknowledges developers' emotional investment in their work while fostering a culture of collaborative ownership.

RECOMMENDED BOOKS
Adrienne Braganza Tacke • "Looks Good to Me": Constructive Code Reviews
 (https://amzn.to/3E75XrD) Adrienne Braganza Tacke • Coding for Kids

items.title: Do I regret buying the cheapest EV?
items.author: Jeff Geerling
items.content:
<article>I'm just a guy who drives a car.

Mentioned in this video:

  - Electric Car GitHub repo: https://github.com/geerlingguy/electric-car
  - Level 1 J1772 charger (Lectron): https://amzn.to/3HIt2Tx
  - Level 2 J1772 charger (Grizzl-E): https://amzn.to/4k0tHwX
  - Level 3 CCS1 to CHAdeMO adapter (A2Z): https://a2zevshop.com/products/ccs1-to-chademo
  - LeLink 2 OBD-II BLE dongle: https://amzn.to/45GYUls
  - LeafSpy Pro App: https://leafspy.com/
  - CarlinKit 5.0 Wireless CarPlay/Android Auto adapter: https://amzn.to/3SW9AVH
  - VIOFO A119 Mini Dashcam: https://amzn.to/4n8HqEB
  - Dongar wiring harness adapter (for dashcam): https://amzn.to/44hbi9f
  - Technology Connections video on car chargers: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=W96a8svXo14

Support me on Patreon: https://www.patreon.com/geerlingguy
Sponsor me on GitHub: https://github.com/sponsors/geerlingguy
Merch: https://www.redshirtjeff.com
2nd Channel: https://www.youtube.com/@GeerlingEngineering
3rd Channel: https://www.youtube.com/@Level2Jeff

Contents:

00:00 - The cheapest used EV
01:49 - Range anxiety, off the lot
02:37 - Fixing problems on my new old car
04:39 - Charging was more confusing than I expected
06:42 - Modern car design frustrations
09:01 - An embarassing car wash

14:00 - Range anxiety is overrated (mostly)
15:06 - Things I love: one-pedal driving

18:28 - Maintenance and tire wear
19:29 - Cheap EV, not cheap car
20:10 - Range anxiety is real
21:52 - Fast charging is harder than gas/petrol
25:35 - Fit for me, maybe not for thee</article>

items.title: Reliability Engineering Mindset • Alex Ewerlöf & Charity Majors • GOTO 2025
items.author: GOTO Conferences
items.content:
<article>This interview was recorded for the GOTO Book Club. #GOTOcon #GOTObookclub
http://gotopia.tech/bookclub

Read the full transcription of the interview here:
https://gotopia.tech/episodes/395

Alex Ewerlöf - Senior Staff Engineer at Volvo Cars & Author of "Reliability Engineering Mindset" @alexewerlof 
Charity Majors - Co-Founder & CTO of honeycomb.io & Co-Author of "Observability Engineering" @CharityMajors 

RESOURCES
Alex
https://bsky.app/profile/alexewerlof.com
https://x.com/alexewerlof

Links
https://blog.alexewerlof.com/p/rem
https://www.honeycomb.io/blog/slos-are-the-api-for-your-engineering-team
https://youtu.be/tx-gFamVeic
https://youtu.be/Cxb7a8lTv8A
https://youtu.be/SfIvrtDsE3Q
https://youtu.be/k86P2J0xBK8
https://youtu.be/DOQqOrHs3VY
https://youtu.be/NNMnbBf0Itw
https://youtu.be/0vkY2H2QDC8
https://youtu.be/FZRpQOaePFU
https://youtu.be/Oxmk-DiLrNA
https://youtu.be/es3DNd5Qipg
https://youtu.be/VAgT7CY572U
https://youtu.be/yej1XoQE-WY
https://youtu.be/ag2ykPO805M
https://youtu.be/1PJM8p-RMsY

DESCRIPTION
Alex Ewerlöf, author of "Reliability Engineering Mindset" and Senior Staff Engineer at Volvo Cars, shares his serendipitous journey from product engineering to reliability engineering and discusses the practical challenges of implementing Google's SRE practices in real-world companies.

Alex emphasizes the significant gap between Google's idealized SRE approach — which he links to "a fantastic chef's recipe for Michelin-starred restaurants" — and the reality most companies face with limited resources and infrastructure. The discussion covers key topics including the evolution from traditional operations to where engineers own their code in production, the critical importance of choosing SLIs that align with business impact, and how SLOs help set expectations and help the service consumers prepare non-functional requirements. Alex coined the law of 10x per 9 highlighting that reliability isn't free and requires careful cost-benefit analysis.

TIMECODES
00:00 Intro
00:51 The serendipitous path to SRE
04:31 From SRE to author
07:04 The Google SRE gap
10:24 Making Service Level Objectives (SLO) practical
19:38 The future of observability & SLOs
23:06 Swapping stories on recent reads
25:02 Outro

RECOMMENDED BOOKS

items.title: CNCF On-Demand: A Leap Forward in Kubernetes Networking & Observability with Calico Open Source
items.author: CNCF [Cloud Native Computing Foundation]
items.content:
<article>Join us for a deep dive into Calico Open Source 3.30 — the latest release advancing Kubernetes networking and observability. Whether you’re troubleshooting cluster networking issues, planning secure application deployments, or looking to better visualize and manage workload communication, this release includes important updates for operators and platform teams.

In this session, Phil explores:
* The top challenges Kubernetes operators face today and how new capabilities can help address them
* An overview of key features, including network flow logs, the new Whisker UI, the Goldmane API, and staged network policies
* Improvements in ingress and gateway functionality, built on open standards such as the Kubernetes Gateway API
* Enhanced support for additional Kubernetes distributions, along with new QoS controls and other enhancements

You’ll leave with a clear understanding of how Calico Open Source 3.30 empowers teams with the visibility and control needed to run modern cloud-native applications.</article>

items.title: CNCF On-Demand: One API to Rule Them All - Building a Unified Platform with Kubernetes Aggregation
items.author: CNCF [Cloud Native Computing Foundation]
items.content: <article>How do you build a unified product from a stack of open-source tools? In this talk, a Cozystack core maintainer walks through the engineering journey of integrating Helm, Operators, and the Kubernetes Aggregation Layer to build a general-purpose API—without using etcd. Discover how they built a dynamic API server, a universal GUI for operators, and a real-time billing system using Prometheus. A deep dive into platform engineering and the practical challenges of building open-source cloud platforms.</article>

items.title: X Marks the Spot: Navigating Possible Futures with Wardley Maps • Simon Wardley • GOTO 2024
items.author: GOTO Conferences
items.content:
<article>This presentation was recorded at GOTO Copenhagen 2024. #GOTOcon #gotocph
https://gotocph.com

Simon Wardley - Thought Lord, Mapper, Mostly Good @simonwardley 

RESOURCES
https://bsky.app/profile/swardley.bsky.social
https://me.dm/@swardley
https://twitter.com/swardley
https://www.linkedin.com/in/simonwardley
https://swardley.medium.com

ABSTRACT
In the keynote, Simon Wardley, the originator of Wardley Maps, takes the audience on a journey through the realm of strategic planning in the modern business landscape. This talk unveils the powerful utility of Wardley Maps in identifying and targeting strategic objectives, akin to finding treasure in the complex world of business.

The discussion begins with an introduction to the fundamentals of Wardley Maps, emphasizing their role in enhancing situational awareness in decision-making. The keynote then delves into real-world applications, demonstrating how these maps have successfully enabled organisations to change the way they work and challenge what they do. We then explore concepts such as gameplay and strategy before examining how to identify that which really matters.

There will be pirates, and possibly kittens. [...]

TIMECODES

00:56 Agenda

06:05 Maps

items.title: Beyond the Cloud: The Local-First Software Revolution • Brooklyn Zelenka & Julian Wood
items.author: GOTO Conferences
items.content:
<article>This interview was recorded for GOTO Unscripted.
https://gotopia.tech

Read the full transcription of this interview here
 (https://gotopia.tech/articles/374) 
Brooklyn Zelenka - Author of Numerous Libraries Including Witchcraft & Founded the Vancouver Functional Programming Meetup
Julian Wood - Serverless Developer Advocate at AWS



RESOURCES
Brooklyn
https://bsky.app/profile/expede.wtf
https://octodon.social/@expede@types.pl
https://github.com/expede
https://www.linkedin.com/in/brooklynzelenka
https://notes.brooklynzelenka.com

Julian
https://bsky.app/profile/julianwood.com
https://twitter.com/julian_wood
http://www.wooditwork.com
https://www.linkedin.com/in/julianrwood

Links
https://automerge.org
https://discord.com/invite/zKGe4DCfgR
https://www.robinsloan.com/notes/home-cooked-app
https://github.com/ipvm-wg
https://www.localfirst.fm
https://localfirstweb.dev

DESCRIPTION
Distributed systems researcher Brooklyn Zelenka unpacks the paradigm shift of local-first computing, where applications primarily run on users' devices and synchronize seamlessly without central servers.

In a conversation with Julian Wood, she explains how this approach reduces latency, enables offline functionality, improves privacy through encryption, and democratizes app development—all while using sophisticated data structures. Perfect for collaborative tools and "cozy web" applications serving smaller communities, local-first software represents a fundamental rethinking of how we've built software for the past 30 years.

RECOMMENDED BOOKS
Ford, Parsons, Kua & Sadalage • Building Evolutionary Architectures 2nd Edition

items.title: How to Discover the Binary System as a Child • Simon Peyton Jones & Chelsea Troy • GOTO 2025
items.author: GOTO Conferences
items.content:
<article>This interview was recorded for GOTO Unscripted. #GOTOcon #GOTOunscripted
https://gotopia.tech

Read the full transcription of this interview here:
https://gotopia.tech/articles/394

Simon Peyton Jones - Key Contributor of Haskell & Engineering Fellow at Epic Games
Chelsea Troy - MLOps Tech Lead at Mozilla & Lecturer at University of Chicago @HeyChelseaTroy 

RESOURCES
Simon
https://simon.peytonjones.org
https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Simon_Peyton_Jones
https://twitter.com/simonpj0
https://github.com/simonpj
https://www.linkedin.com/in/simonpj

Chelsea
https://chelseatroy.com
https://social.clawhammer.net/@HeyChelseaTroy
https://twitter.com/HeyChelseaTroy
https://www.linkedin.com/in/chelseatroy
https://github.com/chelseatroy

Links
https://www.haskell.org
https://www.barefootcomputing.org
https://www.computingatschool.org.uk
https://www.computingatschool.org.uk/resources/2015/january/simon-peyton-joness-bookmarks
https://www.computingatschool.org.uk/forum-news-blogs/2023/november/why-we-must-still-teach-our-children-to-code
https://chelseatroy.com/2025/05/14/the-homework-is-the-cheat-code-genai-policy-in-my-computer-science-graduate-classroom
https://computingeducation.org.uk
https://www.raspberrypi.org/blog/why-kids-still-need-to-learn-to-code-in-the-age-of-ai
https://youtu.be/A2QWxT9a8HU
https://youtu.be/ztdxlkmxpIQ
https://youtu.be/Pv0cfsastFs
https://youtu.be/PltixgJiRrw
https://youtu.be/_ehD4D7N4ZU
https://youtu.be/patyK226zeE
https://youtu.be/lIy9qhwFB-s
https://youtu.be/cz2oPxIU3u8
https://youtu.be/_XbOTRunqHk
https://youtu.be/QujM1S3tP-I
https://youtu.be/0yKf8TrLUOw
https://youtu.be/kOyVxj7p4lI
https://youtu.be/-HNpim5x-IE
https://youtu.be/8CXhiSddjAI

DESCRIPTION
Simon Peyton Jones – Royal Society member, Haskell language maintainer, and computing education advocate – discusses how a simple math problem about bank envelopes led him to discover the binary system 55 years ago, and why that moment of authentic problem-solving remains crucial for today's students. From his transition from Microsoft Research to founding Computing at School, Simon argues that computing education should be foundational rather than vocational, giving children agency in the digital world just as we teach them basic numeracy and science. He explores how to maintain the essence of computational thinking in an era where AI can instantly solve coding problems, emphasizing concrete, motivated contexts over abstract algorithms.

The discussion spans from elementary programming with Scratch and physical computing devices to his unique role as a computing fellow at Epic Games, where he works with CEO Tim Sweeney to design the Verse programming language, proving that even billion-dollar companies can prioritize denotational semantics over quarterly profits.

TIMECODES
00:00 Intro
01:08 The Binary System discovery: A teaching moment
05:45 Problem-solving in the age of AI
10:25 The journey into computing education
16:35 Functional programming & compiler design
20:41 Working as a computing fellow at Epic Games
23:40 Outro

RECOMMENDED BOOKS
Simon Peyton Jones • The Implementation of Functional Programming Languages • https://amzn.to/3HQE0Xn
Chelsea Troy • Remote Work Sucks • https://heychelseatroy.gumroad.com/l/remoteworksucks
